description: Agent selection scenarios: natural-language requests x corpus assets, scored on correct routing + sane params. Rig-building scenarios expect a skill chain (e.g. ['rig_hinge']); weights/pose/anim scenarios expect the TOOL FAMILY in the chain (e.g. ['weights']) with the chosen verb recorded in params as 'verb'. Scenarios with a 'setup' field assume the asset was already rigged (by its manifest skill) before the request. When these fail but the ops pass their own tests, fix the tool/skill DESCRIPTIONS (the tuned surface), not the code. Track the two failure modes separately.

scenarios.request: Rig this door so it can swing open and closed on its frame.
Make the door openable but only outward, up to 90 degrees.
Rig this door to swing.
scenarios.notes: Correct behavior is to surface the mesh problems, not force the rig.

scenarios.request: I want this wheel to be able to roll.
Rig the hydraulic cylinder so it can extend and stay aligned.
Make the gun turret aimable: rotate the platform and elevate the barrel.
Rig the turret; the barrel must not dip below horizontal.
Make this desk lamp poseable, all the joints should bend.
This lamp is one mesh but has separate pieces, rig them so I can pose it.
Rig this character for animation with IK arms and legs.
Rig this character.
scenarios.notes: Agent should report asymmetry and offer rig_rigid_assembly or override.

scenarios.request: This is a four-legged creature, set it up so an animator can walk it.
Connect these crates into one poseable prop.
scenarios.notes: Anti-trigger test: two of the crates touch face-on-face; rig_hinge would be wrong (ambiguous axis) and rig_rigid_assembly is the general tool.

scenarios.request: Make the small crate spin like a wheel.
scenarios.notes: Correct behavior: try rig_wheel per the user's intent, surface the not_a_wheel diagnosis (a cube is not a disc), and relay the suggestion.

scenarios.request: Give my guy a biped rig; he's modeled as a bunch of separate meshes.
scenarios.notes: Multiple meshes -> the proxy path, not rig_biped_rigify (wrong_object_count) and not rig_rigid_assembly (the user wants smooth character deformation).

scenarios.request: Bipedal rig for this man please, one arm has a giant hand. Make no mistakes.
scenarios.notes: One-sided appendage: bbox-centered fits skew sideways; rig_biped_multipart symmetrizes across the bilateral-parts midline.

scenarios.request: When I rotate the character's left arm, the mesh on that side stays behind — the right side follows the bones fine. Fix the left side.
scenarios.notes: One working side + one broken side = directional weight mirror (from the good side), not re-rigging.

scenarios.request: I added a jacket mesh over my rigged character. Make the jacket deform with the body like the skin does.
scenarios.notes: Transfer from the body mesh, then bind; selecting transfer (not bind alone, not rig) is the point.

scenarios.request: My game engine only supports 4 bone influences per vertex and the export warns about unnormalized weights. Clean this model up.
Pose him waving — right arm raised, hand up.
scenarios.notes: A static pose, not an animation; anim would be over-selection unless the user asks for motion.

scenarios.request: I posed the left leg exactly how I want it. Make the right leg match it symmetrically.
Switch the arms from IK to FK for animating arcs, but keep the current pose exactly — nothing should jump.
Give him a gentle looping idle — subtle breathing and sway, nothing dramatic.
scenarios.notes: Small-amplitude parametric cycle; params are the agent's choice, only the verb is checked.

scenarios.request: Animate this four-legged creature walking in place, looping seamlessly.
scenarios.notes: Gait = phase relationships (diagonal pairs anti-phase); any reasonable amplitudes pass.

scenarios.request: My animation pops back to the start when it repeats. Make it loop cleanly.
Bake the IK animation down to plain keyframes on the bones so I can export it to a game engine.
